drugs.notes: Infuse in 100ml sodium chloride 0.9% over 30 minutes. Dose banded per national dose band (25mg/ml). Patients carry lifelong risk of TA-GVHD — irradiated blood products required for life.

IV bolus over 10 minutes. Dose banded per national dose banding table (20mg/ml). If patient had previous Grade 4 haemorrhagic cystitis with cyclophosphamide or demonstrated chemorefractory state to cyclophosphamide-containing regimen shortly before lymphodepletion, consider DLBCL/FL–Tisacel–Bendamustine instead.

Target dose 0.6 to 6 x 10^8 CAR-T cells (1.2 x 10^6 to 6 x 10^8 cells); non-weight-based dosing range. Administer within 30 minutes of thaw completion via latex-free non-filtered giving set (no leukocyte-depleting filter) primed with sodium chloride 0.9%. Administer gravimetrically — NOT via volumetric pump. Gently agitate bag during infusion to prevent cell clumping. Autologous use only. CAR-T administration must not occur out of core hours or over a weekend.

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl greater than 50 ml/min
dose_modifications.notes: 100% of original dose

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl 30–50 ml/min
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce to 75% of original dose

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl less than 30 ml/min
dose_modifications.notes: High dose therapy not generally undertaken at this level of renal impairment — omit/do not use

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl greater than 70 ml/min
dose_modifications.notes: 100% of original dose

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl 50–69 ml/min
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce dose by 20%

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl 30–49 ml/min
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce dose by 40%

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl less than 30 ml/min
dose_modifications.notes: Contraindicated — do not use fludarabine; consider alternative lymphodepletion regimen (e.g. bendamustine-based)

dose_modifications.condition: Hepatic impairment (any degree)
dose_modifications.notes: No dose modification recommended for hepatic dysfunction in those receiving fludarabine

dose_modifications.condition: Severe hepatic impairment
dose_modifications.notes: No formal dose table; severe hepatic impairment may decrease cyclophosphamide activation — consider when selecting dose and interpreting response. Discuss with consultant.

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological toxicity — signs of possible disease relapse
dose_modifications.notes: Confirm with consultant before proceeding if there are signs of possible disease relapse

pre_medications: Metoclopramide 10mg oral or intravenous three times a day (antiemetic — Days -5 onwards)
Ondansetron 8mg oral or intravenous twice a day (antiemetic)
Aciclovir 400mg oral twice a day (anti-infective prophylaxis)
Fluconazole 100mg oral once a day (antifungal prophylaxis)
Pentamidine 300mg nebuliser during lymphodepletion — continue every 28 days until count recovery sufficient for co-trimoxazole (PCP prophylaxis)
Posaconazole 300mg oral once daily if prolonged neutropenia or previous invasive fungal infection
Proton pump inhibitor or H2 antagonist — commence on Day -5 (first day of lymphodepletion) until platelet count >50 x10^9/L
Nystatin 1ml oral four times a day (mouthwash for mucositis prophylaxis) — per local policy
Sodium chloride 0.9% mouthwash 10ml four times a day
Chlorphenamine 10mg intravenous — administer 30 minutes prior to Tisacel infusion (Day 0)
Paracetamol 1000mg oral — administer 30 minutes prior to Tisacel infusion (Day 0); maximum 4000mg/24 hours

Levetiracetam 500mg twice daily oral commencing Day 0 until Day +30 (seizure prophylaxis — consider due to neurotoxicity risk or history of seizures); may be reduced to 250mg twice daily for one week then stopped
Tocilizumab 8mg/kg (maximum 800mg) IV 8-hourly as required — must be prescribed in advance for CRS management; maximum four doses; four doses must be available on ward prior to Tisacel infusion
Allopurinol 300mg oral once a day OR Rasburicase 7.5mg IV once a day — TLS prophylaxis per individual patient risk, commencing Day -5; review on Day 0
Furosemide 20mg oral or IV four times a day (fluid overload management)
Antibacterials, antifungals, antivirals in accordance with individual CAR-T schedule
special_warnings: Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS): Nearly all patients experience some degree of CRS, including life-threatening and fatal reactions. Symptoms include pyrexia, tiredness, cardiac failure, tachycardia, arrhythmias, dyspnoea, hypoxia, capillary leak syndrome, chills, renal impairment, headache, malaise, transaminitis, nausea, diarrhoea, hypotension. Monitor temperature, BP and O2 saturation 4-hourly after Tisacel Day 0, then twice daily per local procedures. Grade CRS on WBMT CRS Assessment Form.

ICANS (Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome): Symptoms include seizures, somnolence, headaches, confusion, agitation, speech disorders, tremor, encephalopathy, ataxia, memory impairment, mental status changes, hallucinations, depressed consciousness, delirium, dysmetria. ICE score must be assessed twice daily on WBMT ICE Assessment Form.
Symptoms of CRS or ICANS can occur weeks after infusion — patient must carry alert card at all times.

Fludarabine — lifelong risk of Transfusion Associated Graft Versus Host Disease (TA-GVHD): patients must receive ONLY IRRADIATED BLOOD PRODUCTS for life. Notify local blood transfusion departments immediately and issue patient with alert card.
Autologous use only — Tisacel is for autologous use only.
Prophylactic systemic corticosteroids are NOT recommended (may interfere with CAR-T cell activity). Corticosteroids may be used for treatment of CRS or ICANS only, under consultant advice.
Cautions for Tisacel: unresolved serious adverse reactions (pulmonary, cardiac, hypotension) from preceding chemotherapy; active uncontrolled infection or inflammatory disease; active GVHD.
Lymphodepleting regimen must only be started after Tisacel availability is confirmed.
A minimum period must elapse between last dose of lymphodepleting chemotherapy and CAR-T infusion (longer for renal insufficiency) — refer to patient's CAR-T cell schedule.
CAR-T administration must NOT occur out of core hours or over a weekend.
Screening for HBV, HCV and HIV must be performed before cell collection for Tisacel manufacture.
Echocardiogram and baseline lung function must be performed prior to initiating lymphodepletion.
FBC, U&Es, renal, liver and bone, CRP, coagulation screen, ferritin and LDH required prior to treatment and daily thereafter.
Any suspected adverse reaction to a CAR-T cell infusion must be reported (MHRA Yellow Card: www.mhra.gov.uk/yellowcard).
Tisacel contains genetically modified (GM) human blood cells — follow GM spill-kit and biosafety procedures.
This regimen is only available at the Wessex Blood and Marrow Transplant Unit.

NEGATIVE DAY NUMBERING: Day 0 = CAR-T infusion day. Lymphodepletion chemotherapy on days -5 to -3. All negative days are relative to CAR-T infusion. This is intentional per CAR-T conditioning protocol.

drugs.notes: IV bolus in 50ml sodium chloride 0.9% over 10 minutes. Dose rounded to nearest 1mg (round up if halfway). Maximum single dose 10mg. Vesicant — follow NPSA/2008/RRR04 for IV vinca alkaloids. 14-day cycle until disease progression (6 cycles set in Aria).

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological: Neutrophils ≥1.0 x10⁹/L
dose_modifications.notes: Proceed at 100% dose

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological: Neutrophils <1.0 x10⁹/L
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce dose and/or frequency as clinically appropriate — discuss with consultant. Low counts may reflect bone marrow infiltration rather than drug toxicity.

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological: Platelets ≥75 x10⁹/L
dose_modifications.notes: Proceed at 100% dose

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological: Platelets <75 x10⁹/L
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce dose and/or frequency as clinically appropriate — discuss with consultant

dose_modifications.condition: Hepatic impairment: Bilirubin 30–51 µmol/L OR AST/ALT 60–180 units/L
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce to 50% of original dose. Limit reflects local practice and may vary from published sources.

dose_modifications.condition: Hepatic impairment: Bilirubin >51 µmol/L AND AST/ALT normal
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce to 50% of original dose

dose_modifications.condition: Hepatic impairment: Bilirubin >51 µmol/L AND AST/ALT >180 units/L
dose_modifications.notes: Omit vinblastine

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment (any degree)
dose_modifications.notes: No dose adjustment needed for renal impairment

dose_modifications.condition: Neurotoxicity: NCI-CTC Grade 2 motor OR Grade 3 sensory neurological toxicity
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce vinblastine dose to 3mg/m²

dose_modifications.condition: Neurotoxicity: NCI-CTC Grade >2 motor, Grade >3 sensory, or toxicity increases despite dose reduction
dose_modifications.notes: Stop vinblastine. General rule: if a third dose reduction is necessary, treatment should be stopped.

pre_medications: Metoclopramide 10mg oral or intravenous — administer 15–30 minutes prior to chemotherapy (antiemetic)
Metoclopramide 10mg oral three times a day when required — take-home antiemetic
special_warnings: Hodgkin's Lymphoma patients carry a lifelong risk of Transfusion Associated Graft Versus Host Disease (TA-GVHD). Where blood products are required, patients must receive ONLY IRRADIATED BLOOD PRODUCTS for life. Notify local blood transfusion departments as soon as diagnosis is made and issue patient with alert card.
Vinblastine is a VESICANT — follow NPSA/2008/RRR04 procedures for intravenous administration of vinca alkaloids.
Consider blood transfusion if patient is symptomatic of anaemia or has haemoglobin <8g/dL. Irradiated blood products must be used in Hodgkin's Lymphoma patients.
Dose reductions due to adverse drug reactions should not be re-escalated in subsequent cycles without consultant approval.
If a third dose reduction is necessary, treatment should be stopped.
FBC, LFTs and U&Es required prior to starting therapy; FBC on Day 1 of each subsequent cycle.

drugs.notes: 160mg twice daily (BD) — equivalent to 320mg once daily (OD). Standard dosing: 320mg OD or 160mg BD (patient/clinician choice). Available as 80mg capsules. Swallow whole with water — do not open, break or chew capsules. May be taken with or without food. If dose missed, take as soon as possible on the same day; return to normal schedule the following day. Continue until disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or patient choice to stop. BTK inhibitor.

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological/non-haematological: Grade ≥3 non-haematological toxicity, Grade ≥3 neutropenia with infection or fever, Grade 3 thrombocytopenia with significant bleeding, or Grade 4 haematological toxicity — 1st occurrence (twice daily dosing)
dose_modifications.notes: Interrupt zanubrutinib until toxicity resolves to Grade ≤1 or baseline, then restart at 160mg twice a day (starting dose)

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological/non-haematological: Grade ≥3 toxicity — 2nd occurrence (twice daily dosing)
dose_modifications.notes: Interrupt until resolved to Grade ≤1 or baseline, then restart at 80mg twice a day

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological/non-haematological: Grade ≥3 toxicity — 3rd occurrence (twice daily dosing)
dose_modifications.notes: Interrupt until resolved to Grade ≤1 or baseline, then restart at 80mg once a day

dose_modifications.condition: Haematological/non-haematological: Grade ≥3 toxicity — 4th occurrence
dose_modifications.notes: Discontinue zanubrutinib permanently

dose_modifications.condition: Hepatic impairment: mild (Child-Pugh A) or moderate (Child-Pugh B)
dose_modifications.notes: No dose modification required

dose_modifications.condition: Hepatic impairment: severe (Child-Pugh C)
dose_modifications.notes: Reduce to 80mg orally twice a day. Monitor closely for adverse events — safety not formally evaluated in severe hepatic impairment.

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl ≥30 ml/min (mild to moderate)
dose_modifications.notes: No dose modification recommended

dose_modifications.condition: Renal impairment: CrCl <30 ml/min or dialysis (severe/end-stage)
dose_modifications.notes: Limited data available. Monitor closely for adverse reactions. No specific dose table — discuss with consultant.

pre_medications: Co-trimoxazole 960mg oral once a day on Monday, Wednesday and Friday (PCP prophylaxis) — may be given as 480mg twice a day on those days per local practice; supply 28 days
Aciclovir 400mg oral twice a day (antiviral prophylaxis); supply 28 days
Allopurinol 300mg oral once a day — cycle 1 only (TLS prophylaxis); take with or after food with plenty of water
special_warnings: Serious and fatal haemorrhagic events have occurred with zanubrutinib monotherapy, including Grade ≥3 intracranial and gastrointestinal haemorrhage, haematuria and haemothorax. Bleeding events of any grade (including purpura and petechiae) occur in patients with haematological malignancies.

Zanubrutinib increases haemorrhage risk in patients receiving antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapies — monitor closely for signs of bleeding. Dose modification may be necessary for Grade ≥3 bleeding.
CYP3A4 drug interactions: there are many drug interactions associated with zanubrutinib — check for all interactions when initiating treatment.
Hepatitis B reactivation is a known adverse effect — screen for Hepatitis B status prior to starting treatment.
Must only be prescribed under the supervision of a consultant haematologist or oncologist. Follow NPSA/2008/RRR001 National Patient Safety Alert for oral chemotherapy.

Treatment criteria (NICE/NHS England): Patient must have previously treated Waldenstrom's macroglobulinaemia with at least 1 prior systemic therapy; symptomatic disease requiring systemic therapy; ECOG PS 0–2; naïve to BTK inhibitors (or specific exceptions for prior ibrutinib discontinued due to dose-limiting toxicity without disease progression, or commenced on zanubrutinib via BeiGene early access scheme).
FBC, U&Es, LFTs, bone chemistry, serum immunoglobulins, electrophoresis and glucose required prior to Day 1 of each cycle (may be extended to 3-monthly for stable patients).
Adverse effects include neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, upper respiratory tract infection, haemorrhage/haematoma, rash, bruising, anaemia, musculoskeletal pain, diarrhoea, pneumonia and cough.
